our_argument_summary: Section 2.1 argues the dome's own cavity geometry H(r)=8537·exp(−r/8619) predicts Schumann frequencies far from the observed 7.83 Hz. It shows pole height gives 8.77 Hz and 'equator' gives ~59 Hz, claims ~22 Hz as a best estimate via averaging, and then argues the dome silently switches to the globe formula. The section also argues that the V12 H(r) 'reconciliation' of three heights (9,572 km Schumann, 9,086 km parameterization, 4,750 km Polaris) is mathematically impossible because the function's supremum is 8,537 km, and the post-hoc radius selection is epistemically empty.

cycle1_fixes_applied.averaging_replaced_with_rigorous: PARTIAL — WIN-002 detail now has exact H_eff integrals (3,316 km linear, 2,140 km area-weighted → 22.6/35 Hz). But the section 2.1 PROSE was NOT updated — it still uses the vague '~22 Hz as a best estimate (averaging pole and equatorial heights)' phrasing that Cycle 1 flagged.
cycle1_fixes_applied.8537_vs_9086_inconsistency: YES — section prose now includes the supremum argument and calls the reconciliation 'mathematically impossible'
cycle1_fixes_applied.silent_switch_citation: PARTIAL — the text says 'He resolves the contradiction by abandoning his geometry and using the globe formula' but still doesn't cite the exact location of the switch (which page or code line)
cycle1_fixes_applied.unfalsifiability_defense: YES — the section addresses the 'different physical mechanisms' defense via the post-hoc radius selection argument

holes_found.description: ARITHMETIC ERROR in equatorial height calculation. Section states: 'At equator (r = 15,000 km), this gives H = 8,537 × e^(−15000/8619) ≈ 8,537 × e^(−1.74) ≈ 1,270 km.' Verified: e^(−1.74) = 0.17552, so 8537 × 0.17552 = 1,498 km — NOT 1,270 km. The 15.2% error propagates to the frequency: c/(4×1498) = 50.1 Hz, not the claimed ~59 Hz. The correct H=1,270 km would require r ≈ 16,423 km, not 15,000 km. A dome defender who runs this calculation catches us making the exact type of error we accuse them of — careless arithmetic — and uses it to discredit the entire section.
holes_found.recommendation: Fix the intermediate values: H(15000) ≈ 1,498 km → f ≈ 50 Hz. OR better yet, implement the Cycle 1 recommendation: replace the vague pole/equator averaging with the precise H_eff integral calculations already present in WIN-002's detail (linear avg H_eff = 3,316 km → 22.6 Hz; area-weighted H_eff = 2,140 km → 35 Hz). The precise integral approach is immune to 'which radius is the equator?' challenges AND avoids the arithmetic error.

holes_found.description: '~22 Hz' CANNOT be derived from the numbers the section shows. The section claims 'The dome's own geometry predicts ~22 Hz as a best estimate (averaging pole and equatorial heights).' But: simple average of heights (8537+1498)/2 = 5018 km → f = 14.9 Hz. Simple average of frequencies (8.77+50.1)/2 = 29.4 Hz. Neither is ~22 Hz. The actual 22.6 Hz comes from the LINEAR RADIAL AVERAGE integral H_eff = H₀L/R(1−e^(−R/L)) = 3,316 km, which is a legitimate physical quantity — but the section never shows this derivation. A reader who checks the arithmetic finds a number (22 Hz) that doesn't follow from the stated calculation method ('averaging pole and equatorial heights'). This is a credibility problem: the right answer (22.6 Hz) obtained by undisclosed methodology looks like the wrong answer obtained by vague hand-waving.
holes_found.recommendation: Show the derivation inline. Replace 'averaging pole and equatorial heights' with: 'The linear radial average gives H_eff = H₀·L/R·(1−exp(−R/L)) ≈ 3,316 km → f = c/(4 × 3,316) ≈ 22.6 Hz; the area-weighted disc average gives H_eff ≈ 2,140 km → 35 Hz. Both are far from 7.83 Hz.' This makes the 22 Hz claim verifiable and shows we actually computed it. Keep the pole/equator bracketing too (8.77 to 50 Hz), but as bounds, not as the source of the estimate.

holes_found.description: 'Equator' radius undefined. The section uses r = 15,000 km for 'equator' without justification. In the dome's azimuthal equidistant projection, the equator is typically at r ≈ 10,008 km (π/2 × 6,371 km). At r = 10,008 km, H(10008) = 2,673 km → f = 28.1 Hz. At r = 15,000, it's 1,498 km → 50 Hz. At disc edge (r = 20,015), H = 838 km → 89.5 Hz. The dome model doesn't clearly define what 'equator' maps to in disc coordinates, and using 15,000 km without citation invites the objection that we picked a radius to make the numbers look bad.
holes_found.recommendation: Either cite where the dome model defines the equatorial radius, or switch to the H_eff integral approach which doesn't require choosing a specific radius. Alternatively, state the full range: 'Anywhere between r = 0 (pole, 8.77 Hz) and r = 20,015 km (rim, 89.5 Hz), the quarter-wave formula never produces 7.83 Hz — the function c/(4·H(r)) is monotonically increasing from 8.77 Hz.' This is stronger because it eliminates all radius-selection ambiguity.

holes_found.description: The section says 'The dome's own geometry predicts ~22 Hz as a best estimate' but then the subsequent paragraphs give a much more sophisticated analysis of the H(r) reconciliation failure (supremum argument, post-hoc radius selection). The opening ~22 Hz claim is now the weakest part of the section, while the supremum argument is bulletproof. The opening creates a vulnerability that the rest of the section has transcended.
holes_found.recommendation: Restructure: lead with the supremum argument ('H(r) can never reach 9,572 km or 9,086 km — mathematical impossibility') and the formula-switching critique, then use the ~22 Hz integral as supporting detail. This front-loads the strongest argument and avoids starting with the most challengeable claim.

stronger_arguments.description: The monotonicity argument is stronger than any averaging approach and is already partially present. f_QW(r) = c/(4·H(r)) = c/(4·H₀·exp(−r/L)) is monotonically INCREASING from f(0) = 8.77 Hz to f(R) = 89.5 Hz. It equals 7.83 Hz at NO real radius (would require r < 0). Therefore the dome's own quarter-wave formula, applied to the dome's own H(r), cannot produce 7.83 Hz anywhere on the disc. This is clean, verifiable, and requires no averaging or radius selection.
sources: Monotonicity of exp(r/L) for positive r and L

stronger_arguments.description: The Q-factor argument from Cycle 1 is still absent from the section prose. Schumann Q ≈ 4–6 (Nickolaenko & Hayakawa 2002). A copper dome ceiling would give Q >> 100. The observed broad, low-Q spectral peaks require a lossy boundary — the ionosphere. This is an independent observable that the section does not mention.
sources: Nickolaenko & Hayakawa, Schumann Resonances in the Earth-Ionosphere Cavity, 2002

kernel_of_truth.description: The dome model correctly identifies that Schumann resonances require a conductive upper boundary, and the 26% gap between ideal and observed Schumann frequency is a real phenomenon in atmospheric electrodynamics that took decades to fully explain.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: The ionosphere IS the conductive boundary, measured by in-situ instruments on sounding rockets and satellites. The dome model's exponential cavity geometry produces the wrong frequency at every radius, and the dome's own supremum (8,537 km) is below the Schumann-derived height (9,572 km) — a mathematical impossibility that no aetheric medium can resolve.

quantitative_verification.computations: Computed H(r) = 8537·exp(−r/8619) at r = 0, 5960, 10008, 15000, 20015. Computed linear radial average and area-weighted disc average of H(r). Verified Schumann frequency from pole, equator, and rim heights. Checked supremum argument. Verified Chapel Hill height discrepancy.
quantitative_verification.discrepancies: TWO ERRORS FOUND: (1) H(15000) = 1,498 km, NOT 1,270 km (section's e^(−1.74) calculation is wrong — correct value is 0.17552, giving 1,498 not 1,270). (2) '~22 Hz as a best estimate (averaging pole and equatorial heights)' — simple averaging of pole/equator heights gives f ≈ 14.9 Hz, not 22 Hz. The 22.6 Hz comes from the linear radial integral average, which is a valid physical quantity but is NOT what 'averaging pole and equatorial heights' describes. All other claims verified correct: pole frequency 8.79 Hz ✓, supremum 8,537 km ✓, 9572 exceeds by 12.1% ✓, H(5960) = 4,276 km ✓, 4750/4276 = 11.1% miss ✓.

citations_verified: H(r) = 8,537·exp(−r/8,619) — confirmed in dome model page and sections.json
Supremum of H(r) = 8,537 km at r = 0 — mathematically verified
f = c/(4h) quarter-wave formula — correctly applied (though wrong physics for this geometry)
Section 2b.3 cross-reference — confirmed exists in part2b html
Section 7 (External Falsification) cross-reference — confirmed Part 7 exists
citations_unchecked: Sentman 1995 DOI 10.1007/978-3-662-03137-4_11 — referenced in WIN-002 detail but not in section 2.1 prose directly; DOI format appears valid but content not verified this cycle

deeper_analysis:
Section 2.1 has evolved significantly since Cycle 1. The H(r) supremum argument and post-hoc radius selection critique are excellent additions — bulletproof mathematical arguments that no dome defender can challenge. However, the opening paragraphs still carry the Cycle 1 weakness: an approximate calculation with two arithmetic errors (wrong H(equator) and unjustified '~22 Hz' claim). The section now has a strong ending built on a weak foundation.

The contrast is stark: the supremum argument (H(r) ≤ 8,537 km is an elementary theorem, so 9,572 km and 9,086 km are impossible) is the strongest single argument in the entire self-contradictions section. But a dome defender encountering the 1,270 km error in the first paragraph might dismiss the rest before reaching the devastating conclusion.

Recommended restructure: Open with the supremum impossibility (the strongest argument), then present the formula-switching critique (strong), then provide the averaging estimates as supplementary detail (weaker but illustrative). Currently the strongest material is buried after the weakest.

This is the same pattern the Cycle 1 holistic review flagged: our review front-loads approximate calculations and back-loads rigorous proofs. Section 2.1 is a microcosm of this structural weakness. The fix is simple: swap the paragraph order and correct the arithmetic.

One concern worth noting: the section currently does double duty — it serves as both the overview of the Schumann self-contradiction AND the detailed argument. WIN-002's detail_evidence has the rigorous H_eff calculations but is only visible when a reader expands the WIN detail. The section prose should be strong enough to stand alone, because many readers will only see the section text (via the Self-Contradictions tab) and never expand WIN-002.
